text.content:
The Permission Door — Design Principles

The Permission Door is Location 13 in the subsurface threshold complex. It is the obstacle between the party and the Final Threshold Landing, and the gateway between Phase 8 (the surface investigation reaching its terminus) and Phase 9 (the descent proper).

What the door is, in design terms

The Permission Door is a civic toll procedure that has been carved into stone. It was built by Aerdy bureaucracy at the height of the Permissive Survey, when the Empire formally recognised certain “descents” as toll-eligible commercial routes. The door does not test arcane knowledge, faith, or worthiness. It tests whether the person standing in front of it has paperwork.

What this means for play

  • It is a paperwork puzzle. Almost everything the party has gathered across Phases 6 and 7 — tokens, registry phrases, route notations, courier marks, even forged inspection writs — was once part of a real bureaucratic transit system. The door rewards the party for treating their evidence pile as a transit packet.
  • It has more than five valid solutions. A party that lacks any single key item should still be able to open it.
  • It rewards evidence gathered in Phases 6 and 7. The Ashen Itinerary phrase, the Kestrel & Reed receipt, the toll tokens, Haskur's admittance note, even the forged Brass Crow stamps — all of these contribute somewhere.
  • It punishes brute force, but it does not prevent it. Forcing the door advances the Structural Failure Clock and wakes a guardian. The party can still get through; they will just pay for it.
  • It punishes wrong phrases. Stating the wrong condition aloud causes the door to shift its internal weights, locking out one method per attempt. After three wrong phrases, only Force and Bypass remain.

Tone

The door should feel bureaucratic, not magical. It does not glow. It does not speak. It does not test the soul. It tests whether you have a receipt. The horror is that this is correct. Someone, at some point, wrote a procedure for descending into the dark, and the procedure works.

What the door does not do

  • It does not reveal what is beyond it. The lintel inscription is the only hint, and it is small and unhelpful.
  • It does not threaten the party with knowledge they shouldn't have. No whispers, no visions, no “you are not ready.”
  • It does not require any single PC. Every party composition can open it.

Carry-forward

The method by which the party opens this door matters in Phase 9. A clean opening leaves the threshold complex undisturbed. A forced opening leaves it audibly damaged — and the Final Threshold Landing's lintel shifts, leaving a thin powder of dust that route-cleaners will read.

Player-Facing Description (Read-Aloud at First Approach)

The passage opens into a small antechamber, perhaps ten feet square. The walls are dressed stone, smooth-fitted, mortar-less. The floor has been swept — not recently, but more recently than the rest of the threshold complex. There is dust, but it is even, not disturbed in patterns of long neglect.

The far wall is the door.

It is not a door in the sense of hinges and a latch. It is a slab of pale grey stone, roughly seven feet tall and five feet wide, set flush into the wall with no visible joint that a knife could find. The slab is marked.

At chest height, seven shallow cuts in a row, the leftmost cut very slightly deeper than the others. Below the cuts, the blackened-hill symbol you have seen at the cairn and on the landing — the same symbol, cleaner here, the soot newer.

Below the symbol, a small recessed socket, perfectly circular, about the size of a large coin or a small medallion.

To the right of the socket, four lines of faint script, weathered but legible to a careful eye. To the left of the socket, six pale shapes that look like the outlines of stones laid against the door at some point in the past — faint discolourations only, not depressions.

Above the slab, set into the lintel, four words in old Aerdy commercial script. They are small. You have to lean close to read them. The dust that catches in the cuts of those words is fresh.

The four words read: Accounts above. Witness below.

What the party can immediately try

  • Push the slab. It does not move. There is no give. It is set with a precision that any stonemason in the party will recognise as civil engineering, not magic.
  • Examine the socket. It is the right size for one of the circular tokens the party has likely found at the cairn or in the Toll Vault.
  • Read the script. Anyone with proficiency in Aerdy commercial-court paperwork (or who has studied the Phase 7 evidence) can read it. The four lines are a procedure.
  • Read the lintel. Four words. Small. New dust in the cuts. Recently re-cut.
  • Look at the pale shapes. They are roughly the size and shape of palm-sized stones. They are arranged in a slightly irregular row, not in a perfectly straight line.

What the party can read on the right-hand script

No sail. No road.

Toll paid beneath.

Road fee waived for under-road permissions.

Show token. Speak condition. Lay tally. Witness given.

The four-line procedure is the door's documentation. It is also, with the right materials, the door's instructions.

GM Explanation

The door is an Aerdy Permissive Survey toll-and-witness gate, built in the mid-to-late 5th century CY (probably between 460 and 490 CY, during the high period of Class VII permissive descents). It uses no magic of its own. It does, however, sit at a threshold that does have residual properties, which the door regulates and channels rather than generates.

How it actually works

The door's mechanism is a balanced-weight system inside the wall. The slab is counter-weighted against four internal stone counters. Each counter releases under a specific condition:

  1. The token socket — when a Tarnsmere descent token is seated correctly, a small bronze plate inside the socket depresses a tumbler. One counter releases.
  2. The phrase tumbler — a hidden listening-tube above the lintel runs to a sounding chamber inside the wall. The chamber contains a stack of light, balanced flats. Saying “No sail. No road. Toll paid beneath.” in audible voice causes the air-pressure variation to nudge the flats in the right pattern. One counter releases. Saying the wrong phrase nudges them in a wrong pattern, locking the counter.
  3. The tally row — placing six route-tally stones (or analogous palm-sized objects) on the left-hand discolourations completes a pressure-plate row at the slab's base. One counter releases.
  4. The witness response — the lintel's “Witness below” phrase is the door's request for an answering acknowledgement. Saying any of the recognised witness-given lines (“Witness given,” “Witness rendered,” or the Aerdy commercial reply “Acknowledged beneath”) releases one counter.

When three of four counters are released, the slab rises silently into a recess overhead. When all four release, it rises faster and resets the system — meaning the next descent can use the same procedure cleanly.

The thing the door knows

The door has been opened, by these conditions, by every permitted descent recorded in the registry. It does not know who is being permitted today; it knows whether they have the paperwork. That is the genuine horror, and it should land at the table: this is an old bureaucratic procedure for descending into something that should not have a bureaucratic procedure.

What the door is keyed against

The door is keyed to six categories of permitted descent, of which the party only needs to satisfy three. The categories are:

  • Token (toll paid).
  • Phrase (transit condition spoken aloud).
  • Tally (route account balanced).
  • Witness (acknowledgement given).
  • Registry (entry attested).
  • Channel (someone with authority opens it for you).

The six Methods (A–F) listed below each satisfy a different combination of these categories, or bypass the door entirely.

The Door's Elements, in Detail

1. Seven shallow cuts

At chest height, seven cuts. The leftmost is slightly deeper. This is not part of the puzzle's mechanical solution — the cuts are an old route-tally mark, identifying this door as a Class VII Tarnsmere permission point. The cuts identify the door; they do not unlock it.

However: the cuts react to certain things. Specifically, the seventh cut (the rightmost) warms slightly in the presence of:

  • A Tarnsmere descent token within 1 foot of the door.
  • Recent route-courier movement (within the last six hours).
  • Kitty's presence — specifically, when Kitty approaches the door within 5 feet. The seventh cut warms distinctly, like a stone left in sun. Other PCs can feel this if they touch the cut at the same time.

This is the Phase 8 payoff of Kitty's chthonic-recognition thread (foreshadowed in Phase 7). The door, or rather the threshold the door regulates, recognises something about her descent. The GM should not explain this; let Kitty's player feel it.

2. Blackened-hill symbol

Below the cuts, the same blackened-hill mark from the cairn and the landing. Re-blackened recently with soot from a route-cleaner brazier. The mark identifies the cargo (or the descent) as “Tarnsmere-bound under permission.” It is a wayfinding aid, not a key.

3. Token socket

Circular, about the size of a large coin. Seats one Tarnsmere descent token. The token sits flat and depresses a bronze plate. This is Method A's primary action. Removing the token after the door opens resets the socket; the token can be re-used.

4. Four-line script (right-hand wall)

The procedure:

No sail. No road.
Toll paid beneath.
Road fee waived for under-road permissions.
Show token. Speak condition. Lay tally. Witness given.

The script is the door's documentation. It can be read by anyone with even passing Aerdy commercial-court literacy. PCs who studied Phase 7 evidence (especially the Ashen Itinerary fragments) recognise the first two lines as the registry phrase.

5. Six pale shapes (tally row)

Six palm-sized discolourations on the floor at the slab's base, arranged in a slightly irregular row. Each shape is the outline of a stone that was once laid here. The shapes correspond to the six route-tally stones kept at most permissive descent points.

The party can satisfy the tally row by laying down any six palm-sized stones in roughly the right pattern. The stones do not need to be the original tally stones. (The original tally stones were probably destroyed in the 542 CY closure.) However, the row needs to be visually approximate.

If the party has gathered the Seven-Cut Cairn's wedge-stones earlier, these are perfect tally stones. Otherwise, anything roughly the right size works.

6. Lintel inscription

Accounts above. Witness below.

This is the door's summary of itself. “Accounts above” refers to the toll account — what the registry tracked. “Witness below” refers to what the descent was for: something below required witnessing, and the procedure required someone to acknowledge the witness in passing through. The inscription is recently re-cut. The route-cleaners are maintaining this door. They expect to use it again.

7. The listening tube (not visible)

Hidden above the lintel, a small opening leads to the phrase-tumbler chamber. A careful Investigation DC 16 spots it. Knowing about it is not necessary to satisfy the phrase condition; speaking the phrase aloud at the door works regardless.

Method A — The Token Method (Clean Opening)

Required materials

  • One Tarnsmere descent token — available from the Seven-Cut Cairn (Surface Location 2, Investigation DC 13) or the Toll Vault (Subsurface Location 8, Investigation DC 15).
  • The phrase: “No sail. No road. Toll paid beneath.”
  • Six palm-sized stones to lay on the tally row.

Procedure

  1. Seat the token in the circular socket. The token clicks softly into place; a faint depression activates a hidden tumbler.
  2. Speak the registry phrase clearly: “No sail. No road. Toll paid beneath.”
  3. Lay six palm-sized stones along the discolourations on the floor at the door's base.
  4. Speak the witness-given response: “Witness given” (or any of the accepted variants: “Witness rendered,” “Acknowledged beneath”).

When all four conditions are satisfied, the slab rises silently into the lintel recess over the course of about ten seconds. The motion is smooth, mechanical, and almost frictionless. The party can see the recess overhead lined with brass guides.

Consequences

  • No Lockdown Clock advance. This is the cleanest opening available.
  • No Structural Failure Clock advance.
  • No guardian wakes.
  • The door remains operable. The party can use the same procedure to come back up.
  • The mechanism resets. Closing the slab is a matter of removing the token and stepping clear; the slab descends slowly over half a minute.

What this requires the party to have done

  • Found at least one token in Phases 7 or 8.
  • Read the registry phrase (in Phase 7's Ashen Itinerary or here in the Broken Registry Chamber).
  • Been observant enough to read the four-line script on the right-hand wall.
  • Kept their nerve at a door that does not appear to require violence.

If only the token is present

If the party seats the token but does not satisfy the other conditions, the door shifts slightly — about half an inch up — and stops. They can hear the internal counters partly disengage. This is feedback. They are partway there. They have not failed. Encourage them to read the wall.

Method B — The Registry Method (Skill-Based Solution)

When this method matters

If the party has no token (the cairn token was lost, the vault token was missed), they can still open the door by treating the procedure as a paperwork problem and solving it.

Required actions

  1. Investigation DC 15 — recover the full registry phrase from the Broken Registry Chamber fragments. The fragments include both the registry phrase and the fee-waiver line.
  2. History DC 15 — understand that the Class VII permissive descent procedure allowed a declared toll account to substitute for a physical token. The history-roller can recite the substitution form: “Permission attested; account standing; toll deemed paid.”
  3. Speak the substitution form aloud at the door.
  4. Speak the registry phrase: “No sail. No road. Toll paid beneath.”
  5. Lay six palm-sized stones on the tally row.
  6. Speak the witness-given response.

The substitution form satisfies the token condition without a physical token. The phrase satisfies the phrase condition. The tally row satisfies the tally condition. The witness response satisfies the witness condition. All four counters disengage.

Consequences

  • No Lockdown Clock advance.
  • No Structural Failure Clock advance.
  • No guardian wakes.
  • The door opens cleanly, but slightly more slowly — the substitution form puts a small additional load on the counter mechanism. The slab rises in about fifteen seconds instead of ten.

Failure to make either DC

If the party fails the Investigation DC by 5 or more, they recover only half the registry phrase and have to fill in the rest by guesswork — this counts as a wrong phrase attempt (see Failed Attempts page).

If they fail the History DC by 5 or more and try the substitution anyway, they speak a malformed form. The door treats this as another wrong phrase. The party should be encouraged to try other Methods first if these rolls are at the edge of viability.

Aid from other PCs

Any PC can Help on the Investigation or History check. Cam in particular benefits if his halfling-network thread is active — he has been reading commercial-court paperwork for weeks; treat his roll at advantage if he describes referring to Hesp Olfair's archive.

What this rewards

This method rewards parties who paid attention to the bureaucratic detail of the campaign. Phase 6 and Phase 7 both seeded the registry phrase, the Ashen Itinerary's transit conditions, and the Permissive Survey's historical framework. A party that took notes on this material rolls these checks well.

Method C — The Kethren Method (Channel Authority)

When this method is available

If Kethren is alive, present, and cooperating — whether genuinely turned, leveraged with the Korre papers, or in custody and choosing to assist in exchange for safe passage out of Greyhawk — she can open the door on the party's behalf. Kethren is a registered route courier; the door's procedure recognises her courier mark.

Procedure

  1. Kethren approaches the door, places her gloved hand flat against the seventh cut (the rightmost shallow cut).
  2. She speaks: “Channel passage. Courier and witness.”
  3. She presses a small bone fingerstamp (her courier mark, the thin vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height) against the blackened-hill symbol.
  4. She speaks the witness response on the party's behalf: “Witness rendered, four parties under courier escort.” (Or however many PCs.)

The door opens. The slab rises in about twelve seconds. No tally row is needed. No token is needed. The phrase is folded into Kethren's recitation.

Consequences

  • No Lockdown Clock advance.
  • No Structural Failure Clock advance.
  • No guardian wakes.
  • However: Kethren now has leverage. She has just opened a Permissive Survey door for the party. She is a named witness to the descent. If the party turns on her in Phase 9 or later, she has a credible threat: “I am the courier of record for your descent. Your names are now on the underside of that lintel. Hurt me and I will see them read by people you do not want them read by.”
  • The route-cleaners learn Kethren channelled the party through. If she is taken alive by them later, this becomes a piece of leverage against her. The GM should make this consequence felt in Phase 9's complications.

If Kethren is dead

This method is unavailable. (Her hand has to do the work. Her courier mark is keyed to her, not to anyone who carries it.)

If Kethren is captured but uncooperative

This method is unavailable unless the party can leverage her into compliance. The Korre papers can do this (Method D), but using them for Kethren consent is different from using them for full channel authority. Refer to Method D for that approach.

What this rewards

A party that captured Kethren alive in Phase 7 and chose not to kill her. Kethren's continued presence has been an active GM resource throughout Phase 8; here, that resource pays dividends. The cost is leverage; the benefit is a clean and immediate opening.

Method D — The Korre Papers Method

When this method is available

If the party has obtained Korre's route-cleaner instructions — either by capturing or killing the Route-Cleaner Lead in the Tollhouse Yard, by trading with Korre directly (if she is still on Phase 7's chessboard), or by recovering them from the Old Guardroom (Subsurface Location 10) — they have access to a sealed packet of paperwork that includes a working forged channel authorization in Korre's hand.

The packet contains:

  • A blank Aerdy commercial-court permission slip, pre-stamped with the “Cindren & Vhal” firm mark (extinct firm; the stamp is genuinely old, recovered from a sealed archive).
  • A handwritten authorisation in Korre's distinctive script: “Bearer permitted descent under route account, witness deferred.”
  • An accompanying tally-substitution scrap with six small inked dots in a row, replacing the physical tally stones.

Procedure

  1. Place the permission slip flat against the door at the level of the four-line script.
  2. Speak the registry phrase: “No sail. No road. Toll paid beneath.”
  3. Place the tally-substitution scrap on the floor where the tally row would be.
  4. Speak the witness response (any variant).

The slip's stamps and the tally-substitution scrap together fool the door's mechanism. The slab rises in about twenty seconds — a little slow, a little reluctant, as if the door is straining to verify something it cannot quite verify.

Consequences

  • No Structural Failure Clock advance.
  • However: a route-notice is triggered. The forged permission slip is, in route-cleaner terms, “notarised on opening” — the door's mechanism reads its stamps and adds the descent to an internal tally that the route-cleaners check. Unless the party also disables the listening tube (Investigation DC 16 to find it, Thieves' Tools DC 15 to plug it) before opening the door, the route-cleaners learn of an unscheduled descent within 8 hours.
  • Lockdown Clock advances +1 if the listening tube is not disabled. This puts the threshold complex into heightened watch for the party's return trip.
  • No guardian wakes.

If the party disables the listening tube first

This is the optimal version of Method D. The forged papers work, the route-notice does not fire, and the Lockdown Clock does not advance. This rewards parties who think about the door's full mechanism, not just its visible elements.

What this rewards

A party that has been aggressive in their Phase 7 dealings with Korre's network — either taking out a Route-Cleaner Lead in Phase 8, or pressuring Korre directly in Phase 7. The papers are a genuine prize and they work, but they leave a paper trail unless the party also thinks about how the door reports its own use.

Method E — The Force Method

When this is the right call

The party has run out of paperwork. The clocks are advancing. Something is coming up the corridor behind them. They need to be on the other side of this door in the next ninety seconds and they don't care what they break to get there.

This is a valid option. The door can be forced. It will hurt.

What forcing the door means mechanically

The slab is approximately 2,400 pounds of dressed stone counterweighted against four internal stone counters. To force the door, the party needs to either:

  • Lever it open from below using a crowbar, prybar, or thieves' tools rigged as a wedge — Strength (Athletics) DC 22, with help from up to three other PCs (the slab's base sits flush; access is difficult). The check can be repeated, but each attempt advances the Structural Failure Clock.
  • Break the counter chain by pulverising the wall to the right of the slab to expose and sever a counter chain — this takes time (10 minutes minimum) and is loud. Any creature within the threshold complex automatically hears it.
  • Use a battering ram or equivalent siege weapon — impractical in the 10-foot antechamber but possible.
  • Magical force — a Knock spell partly works on this door. It releases one counter, which is helpful for Methods A and B if the party is short one condition, but it does not by itself open the door. The door's mechanism is partly mundane and Knock's magical interaction with the listening tube reads as a malformed phrase. Knock on the door costs one attempt's worth of phrase-counter and advances Structural Failure by 1.

Consequences

  • Structural Failure Clock advances +2 immediately on forcing the door open. This puts the threshold complex within one segment of partial collapse on the return trip, and the Final Threshold Landing's lintel shifts, leaving a thin powder of dust that route-cleaners will read on their next sweep.
  • Lockdown Clock advances +1 from the noise.
  • The guardian wakes. See sidebar.
  • The door is broken. It cannot be cleanly closed. The slab now hangs partly open, partly tilted, with visible cracks at the base. The return trip will be obvious to any cleaner who comes through.

The guardian (sidebar)

Forcing the door wakes a small construct — a stone-witness — that has slumbered in a recess to the right of the slab since the door was sealed in 542 CY. The stone-witness is a quasi-magical mason-construct, approximately humanoid, three feet tall, mute, with a stone tablet for a face. It does not speak. It does not attack first. It observes. If the party fights it, use the Stone-Witness stat block (see Phase 8 actors). It has 38 HP, AC 16, and three attacks per round; it is dangerous but defeatable by a 6th-level party. Defeating it is loud and adds another segment to Structural Failure. Avoiding it is also possible — it is slow, and once the party is through the door it does not follow.

What this rewards

Honesty about urgency. A party that has decided this door is in the way and they don't have time for paperwork should be able to make that call and pay for it. The cost is real and lasts: the return trip will be noticeably harder. But the option is real.

Method F — The Bypass Method

When this method is available

If the party has discovered the Cistern Drain (Subsurface Location 11), they have an alternate route that bypasses the Permission Door entirely. The drain runs beneath the door's antechamber and emerges into the Stair Gallery (Location 14), one chamber past the door.

What the bypass requires

  1. Locate the drain access in the Cistern Drain chamber. This is straightforward once the party is in the chamber; the access is a narrow vertical shaft at the far end of the cistern.
  2. Crawl through. The shaft is roughly 30 feet long, narrow (Medium creatures with packs must remove them and pass them ahead), and partly flooded with old standing water (1 to 3 feet, depending on recent rain).
  3. Survive the hazards. The shaft has two hazards:
    • Water surge — if the Structural Failure Clock is 2+, a slow inflow has begun; the shaft floods to 4 feet of water during a crossing. Strength (Athletics) DC 13 to swim through with gear. Failure: drop gear or take 1 segment of exhaustion.
    • Gas pocket — midway through the shaft, a small pocket of stale air with mild swamp-gas. Constitution save DC 12 or take 1 level of poisoned for the rest of the crossing.
  4. Emerge in the Stair Gallery. The shaft empties into a low corner of the Stair Gallery's first landing, behind a stone screen the party can push aside from inside the shaft.

Consequences

  • No Lockdown Clock advance.
  • Structural Failure Clock advances +1 only if water surge fires (i.e., only at Structural Failure 2+).
  • No guardian wakes.
  • The door remains sealed. The party has not opened it. This means: on the return trip, they cannot use the door to come back up. They have to either come back through the Cistern Drain (now harder, because Structural Failure has advanced from their Phase 9 activities), or find a different exit.

What this rewards

Caution and thoroughness. A party that explored the Cistern Drain rather than going straight to the Permission Door has a real bypass option. The cost is exhaustion, a possible poisoned status, and a complicated return trip. This option is especially valuable for parties that don't want to leave a trace at the door.

A note on the bypass and the door

The party can also use the bypass to come back at the door from the other side and open it from within. The door's mechanism works both ways. From the Stair Gallery side, the same procedure releases the slab. This is occasionally useful for retrieval — for example, if the party leaves equipment in the antechamber and wants to recover it cleanly without re-traversing the cistern.

Elle's Iron Key — A Seventh, Personal Method

The thread, in summary

Across Phases 4 through 7, Elle has carried an iron key her order has held since 374 CY. The key has no obvious modern use; it has been passed down through her order's archive as a relic whose original lock was lost. The key is plain in profile, with three modest wards and a small ring at the head shaped like a flat hexagonal coin. The ring is the same size and approximate shape as a Tarnsmere descent token.

The key has been a slow-burn mystery on her character sheet for the entire campaign.

The reveal

When Elle approaches the Permission Door for the first time, the iron key's ring — which she has carried on a leather thong around her neck for years — warms perceptibly against her skin. The ring is the right shape for the token socket. It is also the right metal for a Tarnsmere descent token.

This is not coincidence. Elle's order received the key in 374 CY from a deceased Aerdy permissive surveyor who entered the order in late life as a layperson and died there a few years later. The surveyor's name and history were never recorded in the order's books beyond “came from Tarnsmere; gave us his keys.” The key is one of his.

Mechanical effect

The ring at the head of Elle's key can be unscrewed (Investigation DC 10 to notice the threading, once the warming alerts her) and used as a Tarnsmere descent token. It is functionally identical to a recovered token for the purposes of Method A or any other method that requires a token.

However, the ring is a slightly older mint — 484 CY rather than 487+ CY — and it carries a small additional notation: a tiny etched line on the rim, indicating the original surveyor's personal account number.

What this means narratively

  • Elle's order has been holding a Tarnsmere descent token for nearly two hundred years without knowing it. The surveyor who entered the order was running from Tarnsmere — not from violence, but from what he had witnessed beneath it. He never spoke of it. He gave the order his keys when he joined; the keys were filed and forgotten.
  • Elle is the seventh of her order to carry the key. The previous six carriers passed it on without ever knowing what it opened. She is the first who has knelt at the lock it was made for.
  • This is a moment for her player. The GM should pause the action, let her player see what the ring is, and ask: does she use it? Using it makes the door open. Not using it means she has to find another method. Either choice is character-defining.

Consequences of using Elle's key as the token

  • The door's procedure recognises the older mint and the surveyor's account notation. The door opens with a small additional flourish — a faint mason's-mark warmth in the seventh cut, the smell of cold iron, and (only to Elle) a half-second sense of recognition, as though the door has read a name it has not read in centuries.
  • The door treats this as a Class VII descent under the surveyor's old account. The account is technically still open. Returning under the same key is also permitted.
  • No Lockdown Clock advance. No Structural Failure advance. No guardian wakes. This is a cleaner opening than even Method A — the door treats it as expected.
  • Carry-forward. Elle's player now knows her order has been part of the Tarnsmere story since 374 CY. What she does with that knowledge is her business. The GM should not push.

If Elle is not in the party

This method is unavailable. The thread is dormant. The other six methods all still work.

A small GM nudge

If the party is stuck and Elle's player is engaged but unsure, the GM can prompt: “Elle, the ring is warm. You've been carrying it for years and it's never been warm before.” Let her player decide from there.

Kitty and the Seventh Cut

The thread, in summary

In Phase 6 and Phase 7, Kitty's player saw small flashes of chthonic recognition — brief instances where something deep and old in the campaign world seemed to notice her, without context, without explanation, without payoff. The GM has been seeding this carefully: the cellar of Kestrel & Reed felt familiar; the cairn at the Burned Sweep made her uneasy in a way she could not name; the seventh cut on the cairn (Phase 8, Surface Location 2) warmed slightly when she stood near it.

At the Permission Door

When Kitty approaches within 5 feet of the door, the seventh shallow cut on the slab warms distinctly. Other PCs who touch the cut at the same time can feel it — it is unmistakable, like a stone left in afternoon sun.

What this is, in design terms

The Permission Door does not have a magical interface in its own right; it regulates and channels a residual property of the threshold it sits at. The threshold itself has a kind of memory — not a soul, not a will, but a recognition — for certain patterns of descent.

Kitty's character (whatever the specifics of her background, race, or class as the table has played her) carries one of those patterns. The threshold does not know her; it knows something about her descent. This is not a curse, not a chosen-one moment, not a destiny. It is closer to a match — the way a particular key shape fits a particular lock without being magical.

What the warm cut does

On its own: nothing mechanical. It is a sensation. The GM describes it to Kitty's player privately if possible, or quietly at the table.

However, the warm cut opens an additional dialogue option with the door's mechanism. If Kitty places her hand flat against the seventh cut while another party member runs the door's procedure (Method A, B, C, or D), the door's response is faster and smoother. The slab rises in two-thirds the normal time. The phrase-tumbler chamber's tolerance widens; a slightly malformed phrase still works.

Mechanically: One wrong phrase attempt per door encounter does not lock a counter if Kitty's hand is on the seventh cut.

What the warm cut does not do

  • It does not open the door by itself. Kitty cannot just stand there and have the door open. The threshold recognises her, but the door is paperwork. Paperwork still has to happen.
  • It does not give Kitty knowledge. She does not suddenly understand the descent, the threshold, or what is beneath. She has a sensation. That is all.
  • It does not commit Kitty to anything. The thread continues to be a thread. Kitty's player can choose whether to use the warm-cut option or not.

Narrative consequence

If Kitty uses the warm-cut option, the descent has now formally noted her presence. The Final Threshold Landing's lintel, when the party reaches it, has a tiny additional mark cut into it after they pass: a small notch on the underside, where no one would normally look. The mark is Kitty's. The GM does not need to call attention to it.

In Phase 9, this matters in small ways. Whatever the party meets below knows that a particular shape of descent has come through. What that means is for Phase 9 to reveal.

A GM note on tone

This is one of the campaign's quietest reveals. It should not be played as a chosen-one beat. Kitty is not the chosen one. She is a person whose particular history happens to fit a particular kind of doorway. The threshold doesn't care about her name. It cares about her descent's shape. The horror — and the wonder — is that the campaign world has shapes like this in it.

Failed Attempts and Wrong Phrases

What counts as a wrong attempt

The door tracks four categories: token, phrase, tally, witness. A wrong attempt in any category causes the door's internal mechanism to shift, locking out one method.

Specifically:

  • Wrong token — pressing the wrong-shaped object into the socket (a regular coin, a generic firm mark, a magical talisman the party hopes will work). The socket recognises the shape and politely rejects it; no harm done, but the token method is marked as attempted, and a second wrong token locks the token-counter.
  • Wrong phrase — speaking a malformed version of the registry phrase, the substitution form, or the witness response. The phrase tumbler is sensitive; an incorrect phrase audibly nudges flats in the wrong direction. After two wrong phrases, the phrase-counter locks.
  • Wrong tally — placing the wrong number of stones (fewer than six or more than six), or placing them in obviously wrong patterns (clumped, scattered). The tally row has tolerance for irregular spacing; it does not tolerate count mistakes. One wrong tally locks the tally-counter.
  • Wrong witness — saying a wrong-form witness phrase (“I witness this,” “I declare witness,” or any not-quite-formal version) sometimes works and sometimes doesn't. The door has tolerance here. Two wrong witness phrases lock the witness-counter.

What happens when counters lock

Each locked counter removes one method from the table:

  • Token-counter locked — Method A unavailable, and Elle's iron-key method routes through the same counter (also unavailable). Methods B, C, D, E, F remain.
  • Phrase-counter locked — Method B becomes much harder (skill checks at disadvantage), and Methods A and D become harder (require two correct witness phrases instead of one). Methods C, E, F unaffected.
  • Tally-counter locked — All paperwork methods require an additional skill check (Insight DC 14 to compensate). Methods C, E, F unaffected.
  • Witness-counter locked — All paperwork methods require Kethren's presence (which routes through Method C) or a successful Persuasion DC 18 against the door (the door has rudimentary tolerance for genuine declarations of intent). Methods E and F unaffected.

If three counters lock

The paperwork solutions become effectively impossible. Only Method E (Force) and Method F (Bypass) remain. The party should be told this clearly: “The door has stopped responding to procedure. You can break it, or you can go around.”

How to avoid this

  • Encourage the party to read the wall before they act. The four-line script is the door's documentation.
  • When a PC is about to commit to a wrong phrase, prompt them. “You're about to say ‘No road, no sail’ — the script reads the words in the other order. Want to try again?”
  • Reward Insight checks. An Insight DC 13 against the door (looking for whether a phrase is going to work before committing) lets the GM hint that a phrase is wrong-shaped.
  • Let the party take breaks. The door does not punish thinking time. PCs can step back, regroup, reread, and try again. Only attempts count.

If the party is stuck

If the table is genuinely stuck and frustrated, the GM has three tools, in increasing order of intervention:

  1. Read the four-line script aloud again, slowly. Sometimes players miss it in the volume of door description.
  2. Let Iren or another NPC voice text-message — have Iren ping back a clarifying note about the registry phrase from her Phase 7 research. This is a free hint; it costs nothing in fiction.
  3. Have a PC's player remember a relevant Phase 6 or Phase 7 scene. The GM can simply ask: “You read the Ashen Itinerary in Phase 7. The transit phrase from it — does it come back to you?” This is a metagame reach-around; it is fine to use it.

The door should not break the table's session. The Force and Bypass methods exist for exactly this reason.

Per-Class Contributions to the Door

The Permission Door is designed so that every class in a typical party has at least one useful contribution. The GM should look for the right moment to offer each — especially for PCs whose players are quieter or who have not had a chance to shine in Phase 8 yet.

Rogue

  • Thieves' tools can disable the pressure trigger on the landing stair (Subsurface Location 7) and the listening tube above the door (preventing Method D's route-notice). Both checks DC 15.
  • Sleight of Hand can lift a token from Korre's papers or from an NPC's possession without revealing the action.
  • Investigation with proficiency benefits from rogue Expertise — particularly for finding the hidden token in the Toll Vault.

Wizard / Sorcerer / Warlock

  • Arcana identifies the heat-reactive ink on the burned registry packets (Subsurface Location 9), confirming the route-cleaners are actively suppressing a paperwork trail.
  • Arcana at the door clarifies that the door's mechanism is not magical, which is itself a clue: this is not a wizard's problem.
  • Knock spell partly works (releases one counter); not enough to open alone, but useful in combination with another method that is one condition short.
  • Detect Magic reveals only the faintest residual signature at the seventh cut — not from the door itself but from the threshold it regulates. This is a chance to seed Phase 9 atmosphere.

Cleric

  • Religion identifies the witness-given response as a formal Aerdy commercial-court phrase, not a religious one. Helpful for understanding the door is not a divine puzzle.
  • Insight against the door's procedure helps avoid wrong phrases (DC 13).
  • A ritual (any clerical ritual taking 10 minutes and a verbal component) can satisfy the witness condition in a manner the door accepts. The cleric formally witnesses the descent in their deity's name. This is an alternate path through Method B.

Fighter / Barbarian / Paladin

  • Strength bracing for Method E — the Athletics DC 22 to lever the door open is achievable by a high-Strength PC with Help from up to three others, especially if the party has rigged a lever from a Subsurface Location 10 weapon rack.
  • Combat readiness for the stone-witness encounter if Force is used — the construct is dangerous and a front-line PC should be ready for it.
  • History for a fighter with a military or noble background — the Aerdy commercial procedure may be familiar.

Ranger / Druid

  • Mining sense / stone sense (Survival or Nature DC 14) recognises the dressed-stone construction as civilian-grade mason work, not military or magical. The door was built by civic contractors. This is a small but morale-supporting clue.
  • Animal handling / familiar use for sending small scouts ahead through the Cistern Drain (Method F).
  • Survival identifies the swamp-gas pocket in the cistern crawl before the Con save is forced.

Bard

  • Social reconstruction — a Performance or Persuasion DC 13 lets the bard recite the registry procedure in a way that fits the door's tolerance. The bard can speak in the right cadence, the right vocal register, the right Aerdy commercial-court formality. This grants the next phrase attempt advantage.
  • Cutting Words against the stone-witness (if Force is used).
  • Lore — bards with Knowledge or History proficiency are very good at the Method B substitution-form roll.

Artificer / Mason / Inventor types

  • Mason's tools can read the door's stonework and identify, with a DC 14 check, where the counter chains run inside the wall. This is critical for Method E (locating where to pulverise the wall) or for surreptitiously disabling a counter without opening the door (advanced, GM discretion).
  • Tinker's tools / smith's tools in combination with the Toll Vault's bronze mold can cast new tokens from melted brass or tin (a slow solution: 6–8 hours, requires a heat source, makes noise).
  • Artificer infusions on a humble object (a coin, a button) can give it just enough “token-like” weight and stamp to fool the socket. This is a one-shot solution, and the door catches it on the second attempt.

Monk

  • Stillness of mind / patience — monks are particularly good at the “think before acting” aspect of this puzzle. Mechanically, give monks Insight at advantage when reading the door.
  • Slow Fall can be the difference between getting hurt and not getting hurt in the cistern crawl if a PC slips.
  • Athletics for the Cistern Drain crossing — monk Strength tends to be high for the level.

The GM's job here

You do not need to remember every entry on this page. The point is: at this door, no class is just standing around. If a player is quiet during the door scene, look at their class on this page and offer them a contribution.

pages.name: GM Flow for the Door Scene

text.content:
GM Flow for the Door Scene

Pacing

The door scene should run 15 to 35 minutes of real table time, depending on the party's preparation. Less than 15 minutes feels anticlimactic. More than 35 minutes risks frustration. If you sense the scene running long, intervene with Iren's voice ping or a Kitty-seventh-cut nudge.

Step 1 — Arrival and description (3 to 5 minutes)

  1. Read aloud the player-facing description (see Page 2).
  2. Pause. Let the party look around, ask questions, read the wall.
  3. Encourage examination before action. Reward Investigation, History, Arcana, and Religion checks with the corresponding clues.

Step 2 — Inventory check (2 to 4 minutes)

Ask the table to recall what they have that might be relevant. Have each PC name one item or piece of paperwork they would consider bringing to a permissive descent door. The list should include:

  • Any descent token they recovered (cairn, vault).
  • The Ashen Itinerary phrase (from Phase 7).
  • The Korre papers (if obtained).
  • Kethren (if present and cooperating).
  • Elle's iron key (if Elle is in the party).
  • The fee-waiver line (from the Broken Registry Chamber).

Step 3 — Commitment to a Method (3 to 8 minutes)

The party debates and commits. The GM does not intervene unless asked. Let the table choose. If the table is split, give the loudest voice 30 seconds to make their case and then ask everyone for a yes/no.

Step 4 — Execution (5 to 15 minutes)

Run the chosen Method. Roll for the skill checks. Speak the phrases. Lay the stones. Watch the slab rise.

For Methods A, B, C, D, and Elle's key: this should feel like the door is responding to them. The mechanism is described in terms of subtle clicks, faint depressions, soft tumbler-shifts. The slab rising should be a small triumph.

For Method E (Force): this should feel like the wall is fighting back. The stone-witness is a real combat. The dust and noise carry.

For Method F (Bypass): this should feel like a slow crawl through someone else's plumbing. The cistern is genuinely uncomfortable.

Step 5 — Through (2 to 3 minutes)

Once the party is on the far side of the door, transition to the Stair Gallery (Subsurface Location 14). Describe the opening: a long stair descending into a wider gallery, with landings at regular intervals.

Note whether the door is open behind them or closed. Closed (Methods A, B, C, D, Elle, F): the slab descends smoothly behind them, sealing the threshold. Open (Method E): the slab is wedged, broken, leaning. Anyone coming after them will see exactly what happened.

Step 6 — Record (1 minute, end of session)

The GM notes which Method was used. This carries forward into the endings package and into Phase 9. Specifically:

  • Method used — A / B / C / D / E / F / Elle.
  • Lockdown Clock advance — 0 or 1 segment.
  • Structural Failure Clock advance — 0, 1, or 2 segments.
  • Was Kethren channelled in? — Yes / No (only matters for Method C).
  • Was the listening tube disabled? — Yes / No (only matters for Method D).
  • Did Kitty touch the seventh cut during the opening? — Yes / No (carries to Phase 9 atmosphere).
  • Did Elle use her key? — Yes / No (carries to Phase 9 character beats).

The feeling you are aiming for

By the time the party is through the door, they should feel two things:

  1. Quiet pride. They solved a real puzzle. They earned this.
  2. Cold unease. The door was a bureaucratic procedure. Someone wrote a procedure for going beneath the world. And it works.

That second feeling is the Phase 8 endgame in miniature. The party has not yet seen what is below. They have, however, just confirmed that what is below has been formally administered — and that administration is still on file.
